// Layer 2 · Data Link Layer · Network Fundamentals EP.9

HOW
SWITCHES
WORK

A Switch is a Layer 2 device that intelligently forwards Ethernet frames using MAC addresses — replacing the Hub and enabling full-duplex communication between multiple devices simultaneously.

01
// Hub → Bridge → Switch

WHAT IS A SWITCH?

Switches evolved from Hubs and Bridges. A Hub blindly copies every frame to every port — slow and collision-prone. A Bridge divided the collision domain. A Switch combines the best of both: intelligent per-port forwarding with full-duplex, high-speed connections to every device.

HUB (Layer 1)
Floods everything to all ports. Half-duplex — only one device can transmit at a time. Creates a single collision domain. Every device competes for the wire.
BRIDGE (Layer 2)
Connects two HUB segments. Learns which MACs are on which side. Filters traffic between segments — reduces collisions. Still half-duplex within each side.
SWITCH (Layer 2)
Intelligent per-port forwarding. Full-duplex — send and receive simultaneously. Each port is its own collision domain. Learns MACs, forwards only to the right port.
GIF 1 — HUB floods ALL ports vs SWITCH forwards only to destination

HUB CHARACTERISTICS

  • Operates at Layer 1 (Physical)
  • Half-duplex — one direction at a time
  • All ports share one collision domain
  • All ports share one broadcast domain
  • No intelligence — just repeats signals
  • Causes many collisions with more devices

SWITCH CHARACTERISTICS

  • Operates at Layer 2 (Data Link)
  • Full-duplex — simultaneous send & receive
  • Each port = its own collision domain
  • All ports share one broadcast domain
  • Learns MAC addresses → MAC Address Table
  • Forwards frames only to the correct port
💡 REAL-WORLD ANALOGY
Hub = Shouting in a room
Everyone hears everything. Only one person can speak at a time or it becomes chaos (collision).
Switch = Telephone exchange
Messages go directly and privately from sender to receiver. Many conversations happen simultaneously.
02
// Broadcast Domain & Collision Domain

BROADCAST & COLLISION DOMAINS

Two fundamental concepts define how traffic spreads across a network. Understanding these tells you how to design LANs efficiently.

BROADCAST DOMAIN
A group of devices that receive each other's broadcast frames (sent to FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF). All devices in the same broadcast domain hear every broadcast. Routers separate broadcast domains.
COLLISION DOMAIN
A group of devices that can cause a collision when they transmit simultaneously. In a Hub, all ports share one collision domain. Switches give each port its own collision domain — eliminating collisions.
GIF — HUB vs SWITCH vs ROUTER: Broadcast & Collision Domain comparison
DEVICE BROADCAST DOMAINS COLLISION DOMAINS LAYER DUPLEX
Hub 1 (all ports share) 1 (all ports share) L1 Half-duplex
Switch 1 (all ports share) 1 per port (isolated) L2 Full-duplex
Router 1 per interface 1 per interface L3 Full-duplex
Key Rule:  Switches reduce collision domains but cannot separate broadcast domains. Only a Router can stop broadcasts from crossing between networks.
03
// Layer 2 Frame Fields

ETHERNET FRAME STRUCTURE

Every piece of data crossing a Switch is wrapped in an Ethernet Frame. The frame header tells the Switch exactly who sent it and who should receive it — using MAC addresses at Layer 2.

GIF 2 — ETHERNET FRAME: Fields assemble, travel to Switch, get inspected
Preamble Destination MAC Source MAC Ethertype Data (Payload) CRC / FCS
8 bytes
10101010...		 6 bytes
E4:A7:A0:C9:76:C9		 6 bytes
34:A7:11:C9:66:33		 2 bytes
0x0800 = IPv4		 46–1500 bytes
IP + TCP + App data		 4 bytes
Error check
Sync timing Where to send Who sent it Protocol type The actual data Integrity check
Switch uses:  Only the Destination MAC to decide which port to forward the frame out of. The Source MAC is used to learn which port that device is connected to.
04
// Layer 2 Physical Address

MAC ADDRESS

Every network interface (NIC) in the world has a unique MAC address — a 48-bit (6-byte) hardware address burned into the chip by the manufacturer. It is the "real name" of every device at Layer 2.

MAC ADDRESS FORMAT — MM:MM:MM:SS:SS:SS
OUI — Organizationally Unique Identifier
MM : MM : MM
First 24 bits — identifies the manufacturer (assigned by IEEE). E.g. Cisco, Intel, Apple all have unique OUI codes.
NIC — Network Interface Controller Serial
SS : SS : SS
Last 24 bits — unique serial number for each device, assigned by the manufacturer. Guarantees global uniqueness.
Example:  34-A7-11-C9-66-33  →  OUI = 34-A7-11 (Intel)  |  Serial = C9-66-33
ipconfig /all — finding your MAC address (from video)
C:\Users\phornchai.i> ipconfig /all
 
Wireless LAN adapter Wi-Fi:
 
   Connection-specific DNS Suffix:
   Physical Address . . . . . . . .: 80-80-CD-17-36-F8 ← MAC Address (Physical Address)
   DHCP Enabled . . . . . . . . . .: Yes
   IPv4 Address . . . . . . . . . .: 192.168.1.53
 
C:\Users\phornchai.i>

MAC ADDRESS TYPES

  • Unicast: Sent to one specific device (normal traffic)
  • Broadcast: FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF — sent to ALL devices
  • Multicast: 01:00:5E:xx:xx:xx — sent to a group

KEY FACTS

  • 48 bits = 12 hex digits
  • Written as: AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF or AA-BB-CC-DD-EE-FF or AABB.CCDD.EEFF
  • Called "Physical Address" in Windows
  • MAC addresses are used only within a single LAN segment
  • Routers use IP addresses — not MACs — to route between networks
05
// MAC Address Table Behaviour

LEARNING · FLOODING
FORWARDING · FILTERING

When a Switch starts up its MAC Address Table is empty. It builds the table automatically through 4 behaviours — all happening in real time as frames arrive.

①
LEARNING
Switch reads the Source MAC of every arriving frame and records: this MAC is on this port. The MAC Address Table fills up automatically.
②
FLOODING
If the Destination MAC is unknown (not in the table), the Switch floods the frame out all ports except the incoming port. Someone will answer.
③
FORWARDING
If the Destination MAC is known, the Switch sends the frame only to the correct port. Efficient, targeted delivery — no wasted bandwidth.
④
FILTERING
If Src and Dst are on the same port, the frame is dropped (filtered). No need to send it anywhere — they share the same segment.
GIF 3 — MAC TABLE: Learning → Flooding → Forwarding cycle with PC1, PC2, Printer, Server

STEP-BY-STEP EXAMPLE (from video)

STEP 1 — PC1 → PC2 (table empty)
  • Frame arrives: SRC=AAAA.AAAA.AAAA, DST=BBBB.BBBB.BBBB
  • LEARN: record AAAA.AAAA.AAAA → E0
  • FLOOD: DST unknown → send out E1, E2, E3
  • PC2 receives it and replies
STEP 2 — PC2 replies to PC1
  • Frame arrives: SRC=BBBB.BBBB.BBBB, DST=AAAA.AAAA.AAAA
  • LEARN: record BBBB.BBBB.BBBB → E1
  • FORWARD: DST known (E0) → send only to E0
  • PC1 receives the reply directly
STEP 3 — PC1 → PC2 again (now known)
  • Frame: SRC=AAAA, DST=BBBB — both in table now
  • Table: E0=AAAA, E1=BBBB, E2=CCCC, E3=DDDD
  • FORWARD: send directly to E1 only
  • No flooding — efficient direct delivery
MAC TABLE AGING
  • Dynamic entries expire after 300 seconds (5 min) by default
  • Static entries never expire
  • Use show mac-address-table to inspect
  • Use clear mac-address-table dynamic to flush
9Hua-Switch# show mac-address-table (from video slide 16)
9Hua-Switch# show mac-address-table
          Mac Address Table
------------------------------------------
  Vlan   Mac Address      Type    Ports
  ----   -----------      --------  -----
  All    0008.a445.9b40   STATIC  CPU
  All    0100.0ccc.cccc   STATIC  CPU
  All    0100.0cdd.dddd   STATIC  CPU
  1      0008.e3e8.0440   DYNAMIC Fa0/2
Total Mac Addresses for this criterion: 5
9Hua-Switch#
06
// Cisco IOS — Basic Switch Setup

SWITCH CONFIGURATION

A new Cisco switch needs basic configuration before it can be managed remotely. These commands from the video cover hostname, password security, management IP, and SSH access.

① HOSTNAME

Enter privileged EXEC mode, then global configuration mode to set the device name.

Switch — Hostname Configuration
Switch>enable
Switch#configure terminal
Switch(config)#hostname 9Hua-Switch
← Prompt changes immediately to new hostname
9Hua-Switch(config)#exit
9Hua-Switch#

② LINE VTY — TELNET PASSWORD (less secure)

Set a password on VTY lines 0–4 to allow Telnet access. Telnet sends data in plain text — use SSH instead for production.

VTY Line Password (Telnet)
9Hua-Switch(config)#line vty 0 4
9Hua-Switch(config-line)#password 4321
9Hua-Switch(config-line)#login
9Hua-Switch(config-line)#exit

③ SSH — SECURE SHELL ACCESS (recommended)

SSH encrypts the management session. Requires a username, domain name, and RSA key generation.

SSH Configuration (from video)
9Hua-Switch(config)#username admin privilege 15 password cisco
9Hua-Switch(config)#ip domain-name ninehua.com
9Hua-Switch(config)#crypto key generate rsa
How many bits in the modulus [512]: 768
9Hua-Switch(config)#ip ssh version 2
9Hua-Switch(config)#line vty 0 4
9Hua-Switch(config-line)#login local
9Hua-Switch(config-line)#exit
9Hua-Switch(config)#

④ MANAGEMENT IP ADDRESS — VLAN 1

A Layer 2 Switch needs an IP only for management (Telnet/SSH). The IP is assigned to the VLAN 1 interface (SVI — Switched Virtual Interface).

IP Address on VLAN 1 + Default Gateway (from video slide 14)
9Hua-Switch(config)#interface vlan 1
9Hua-Switch(config-if)#ip address 10.5.5.11 255.255.255.0 ← Management IP
9Hua-Switch(config-if)#no shutdown
9Hua-Switch(config-if)#exit
9Hua-Switch(config)#ip default-gateway 10.5.5.1 ← Router IP for mgmt traffic
9Hua-Switch(config)#
Why VLAN 1?   A Layer 2 switch doesn't route traffic — it has no "real" interfaces with IPs. The VLAN 1 SVI is a virtual interface used only for management access (Telnet/SSH/Ping to the switch itself). Normal traffic forwarding doesn't need any IP.

LAYER 2 vs LAYER 3 SWITCH

L2 SWITCH

  • Forwards frames using MAC addresses
  • Has a MAC Address Table
  • No routing between subnets
  • One IP only for management (VLAN 1)
  • Two types: Unmanaged (plug-and-play) and Managed (configurable)
  • Examples: Cisco 2960, most home switches

L3 SWITCH

  • Can forward using both MAC and IP addresses
  • Has both MAC Table AND Routing Table
  • Routes between VLANs (inter-VLAN routing)
  • Multiple IP interfaces (one per VLAN)
  • More expensive — used in distribution/core layer
  • Examples: Cisco 3560, 3750, 4500
07
